text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 09 April 2019

Dr A. Patel
Riverside Medical Practice

12 Riverbank Way
London SE1 5AB
Dear Dr A. Patel
Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1955, Hospital No: H937521 NHS No. 8421905630
Flat 3, 28 Willow Street, London, SE1 3JT 

Diagnoses/summary: History of recurrent unprovoked seizures of uncertain character and cause. Multiple routine EEGs previously nondiagnostic. No clear structural lesion identified on prior brain imaging per patient report.

Prior medication trials (per patient and records): Sodium valproate (sedation and tremor), carbamazepine (skin rash), levetiracetam (irritability), lamotrigine (insufficient benefit), clobazam PRN (helpful for clusters but daytime drowsiness). No history of topiramate, zonisamide, lacosamide, perampanel, or brivaracetam use.

Comorbidities: Mild hypertension (on amlodipine). No known cardiac murmur. No history of head injury or CNS infection. No family history of epilepsy recalled.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He is a newly retired secondary-school teacher living alone. He reports a predictable daily routine with early mornings, scheduled walks, and reduced social interaction since retirement. He keeps a seizure diary on paper and on his phone. He describes episodes that remain variably stereotyped. He notes that over the course of most calendar cycles he will have several events, sometimes in loose clusters, with longer quiet spells in between. He associates poorer sleep and missed meals with increased likelihood of events. He denies tongue biting or prolonged post-event confusion in every instance, though on occasion he experiences notable fatigue afterwards. There is no clear photic trigger, and he does not drive.

Current medication:
- Amlodipine 5 mg once daily
- Clobazam 10 mg at night PRN for clusters (used sparingly)
- Vitamin D 1000 IU once daily

Examination today was unremarkable. Weight stable. No focal neurological deficits. Blood pressure 138/82. He brought recent home BP readings which are broadly similar. Diary review suggests several events spread across most months, typically brief, with occasional back-to-back occurrences on successive days. No injuries reported.

Investigations and plans:
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D level, thyroid function.
- Arrange MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) to update prior imaging from several years ago, as records provided were incomplete.
- Discussed treatment options, including introducing a different anti-seizure medicine. He wishes to consider this after reviewing the MRI and bloods. We also discussed safety at home given that he lives alone: kettle use seated, avoiding high ladders, and considering a wearable alert device. He has already placed pans on back hobs and keeps the bathroom door unlocked.
- Agreed to continue clobazam PRN for short clusters only and to maintain a detailed seizure diary noting sleep, meals, and stressors.
- Provided information on local peer support groups; he will explore options that fit his routine to mitigate social isolation.

Follow-up:
- Telephone review by epilepsy nurse in 6–8 weeks to check diary, adherence, and side effects if any changes occur.
- Face-to-face clinic review in 3–4 months or sooner if events increase in intensity, duration, or lead to injury.
- Emergency plan discussed: seek urgent help if an event lasts longer than 5 minutes or if there are repeated events without full recovery.

Thank you for your continued care and support with blood pressure monitoring and general health maintenance. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Diary review suggests several events spread across most months

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes ongoing seizures without a precise numeric count. Key frequency statements: "over the course of most calendar cycles he will have several events" and "Diary review suggests several events spread across most months". This indicates recurrent seizures in most months with several events but no exact number. Clusters are mentioned but not quantified, so a cluster-based label cannot be formed. Therefore, the best normalized label is the vague frequency 'multiple per month'.
seizure_frequency_number: Diary review suggests several events spread across most months

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per month) disagrees with Reference (1 per year)
